# 0150 — contracts are invariants for functions: `requires`/`ensures` reuse the invariant predicate, checked at the dev/test call site and generated by the runner, stripped from release

- **Status:** Accepted (v0.115; 2026-07-02)
- **Provenance:** the v0.115 increment — the testing track's fourth slice, the rung that makes the *invariant↔test duality* real for pure functions. It is the load-bearing record for function contracts; it also settles the track's open runtime-default question (DECISION J) as one of its decisions.
- **Realises:** the testing track's pillar — *a claim about one result is a contract, checked everywhere for free; a property earns its keep only when the claim is relational or spans calls.* `ensures` is the per-call postcondition; `requires` is the precondition that both guards the call and filters generation.
- **Relates:** the one predicate surface (ADR 0144 — a contract clause *is* the invariant predicate, now attached to a function); the generation record (ADR 0149 — `requires` filters the same refinement-domain inhabitant space `for all` draws from, exactly as a `where` does, and `ensures` violations shrink like a property); the capability-requirement provenance record (ADR 0127 — the source-level `@requires` annotation is a **different** construct, naming the capabilities a handler needs; the value contract here is a bare clause over parameters/`result`, and the two must not be conflated).

## Context

Bynk already carries a *snapshot* invariant on an agent — a named, pure `Bool`
predicate over state that must hold of every committed state, runtime-checked at the
commit boundary. A function has the same shape of claim available: a precondition
its arguments must satisfy, and a postcondition its result must satisfy. The
question was whether functions should get a *new* assertion construct, or reuse the
invariant predicate.

Two forces pointed at reuse. First, ADR 0144 already committed the language to *one
predicate surface* — every checked claim is the invariant predicate. A second
contract grammar (a matcher DSL, a Hoare-triple syntax) would betray that. Second,
Bynk's generator (ADR 0149) already draws valid inhabitants of a parameter's type;
a postcondition over generated arguments *is* a property — a contract is a property
that is always on, so the runner can check it without the author writing a
`property` at all.

A separate open question the track carried (DECISION J): should contracts run at
runtime by default? A contract that runs in production is a safety net but also a
cost and a behaviour change; one that never runs is only a test.

## Decisions

**D1 — a contract is the invariant predicate attached to a function.** A pure `fn`
carries any number of named clauses between its return type and body:
`requires <name>: <pred>` (a precondition over the parameters) and
`ensures <name>: <pred>` (a postcondition over the parameters **and** `result`).
Each predicate is the invariant predicate verbatim — pure `Bool`, `implies`, `is`,
operators, pure methods; no effects, capabilities, `expect`, or `Val`. No new
grammar, no matcher library. Impurity is `bynk.contract.impure_predicate`; a
non-`Bool` clause is `bynk.contract.not_bool`; two clauses sharing a name is
`bynk.contract.duplicate_name`.

**D2 — `result` is a contextual binding, special only inside an `ensures`.** In an
`ensures` predicate `result` names the function's return value (the awaited element
for an `Effect` return); everywhere else it is an ordinary identifier, so existing
code naming a value `result` still parses. `result` in a `requires` is a scope
error (`bynk.contract.result_in_requires`) — the return value is not bound on
entry. (Same contextual trick as `for`/`all` in the generation slice.)

**D3 — a contract is checked at two points, and only there.** (1) A **dev/test-build
call-site guard**: the emitter wraps a contracted function so every call checks each
`requires` on entry and each `ensures` on exit, throwing a contract failure that
names the clause and the offending arguments/`result`. O(1) in code size (one guard
around the definition, not per call site). (2) A **runner attack**: for every
contracted `fn` reachable from a test target, the runner generates arguments over
the parameters' refinement domains (the ADR-0149 engine — boundary-inclusive,
seeded, shrinking), **filters** them by the conjunction of `requires` (exactly as a
`for all … where` filters — inputs failing a precondition are discarded, not
counted), calls the function, and the dev/test guard asserts the `ensures`. A
failure reports the case count, the seed, and a shrunk counterexample with the same
reproduce line a `property` gives.

**D4 — `requires` filters generation; it never fabricates.** The runner reuses the
`where`-filter path: draw the argument tuple, apply the `requires` conjunction as
the acceptance filter, then call. A `requires` unsatisfiable over the domain starves
the attack the way an over-narrow `where` does — surfaced as a warning, never a
false green. No new engine — only wiring the preconditions into the filter the
generation slice already runs.

**D5 — contracts are per-build-profile: on in dev/test, off in release (settles
DECISION J).** `bynkc test` and `bynkc test --inspect` compile with the call-site
guard; the deploy build (`bynkc compile`) strips it entirely — zero runtime cost,
no production behaviour change. A drift-guarded fixture asserts the deploy emit
contains no contract check. An explicit `--contracts on|off` override is a named
follow-on. The runner attack is emitted only alongside the guard (they are always
paired), since the attack relies on the guard to assert `ensures`.

**D6 — a test that merely restates a contract is flagged, conservatively.** A claim
about one result belongs in `ensures` (checked everywhere, generated for free); a
`property` earns its keep only when the claim is relational or spans calls
(monotonicity, round-trips). A `case`/`property` that re-checks a clause already
declared at the source is redundant and flagged
(`bynk.contract.restated_by_test`) — but the check is **conservative**: it fires
only when the test predicate is syntactically / α-equivalent to a declared clause
over the same bound arguments. Under-flagging is acceptable; over-flagging is not
(same posture as the refinement-restatement flag).

## Consequences

- A one-result claim is written once, as a contract, and is checked at every real
  call in dev/test **and** generated against by the runner — the author never writes
  a `property` for it.
- Contracts add no production cost or behaviour: the guard is provably absent from
  the deploy emit.
- The two enforcement points share one lowering of each clause, so a contract can
  never pass one and fail the other.
- Contracts attach to **pure `fn`s** in this slice — the construct the generator can
  attack directly. Contracts on capability operations / handlers (their inputs
  include the world) are dev-guard-only or scenario-`case`-checked, and are a named
  follow-on. The runner attack targets primitive-parameter functions reachable from
  a test target; composite-parameter and effectful-return contracts are dev-guard-
  only for now.
- **Re-openable:** contracts on handlers/capability operations; an explicit
  `--contracts` override; contracts as extractable reference documentation; and a
  full semantic (not syntactic) restatement check — each a named future, none
  blocking v1.
